title: Repair Before Veto, When Repair Is Hidden: Quantum-Accessible Features for Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning
abstract: Hard-constraint decision systems usually veto infeasible candidates. This is too rigid when the system can act: if a known affordable repair would make an infeasible candidate feasible and valuable, rejection is a false veto rather than a ranking error. We introduce Q-RACL (Quantum Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning), a repair-before-veto framework that first defines RACL decision semantics and then identifies the single inference link where quantum feature access can be load-bearing. RACL accepts a candidate when a sequential repair plan restores feasibility and preference; otherwise it returns structured rejection credit. The hard link is repair-feasibility inference: which repair class restores feasibility from an observed candidate and context. We construct a discrete-logarithm-hidden RACL family where the repair class is a shifted interval rule in the latent exponent a = log_g(x), while the learner observes only x = g^a mod p. Under standard DLP-based learning separation, this coordinate is inaccessible to efficient raw-input classical policies but accessible to a quantum agent through Shor/Fourier structure. Across six primes and ten seeds, bounded raw-input classical policies and a wrong raw-Fourier encoding remain near chance, whereas the Q-DLP policy keeps false-veto rate below 1.1%, wins all paired seeds, and yields QNI_cond = 0.9777 to 0.9972. A classical DLog oracle matches it, isolating feature access rather than classifier capacity. Thus quantum AI is not added as a generic model upgrade; for this DLP-hidden repair family, it supplies the missing feature that closes the repair-before-veto loop.
summary: This paper introduces Q-RACL, a decision framework that mandates checking for possible repairs before rejecting candidates. By encoding this check into a Discrete Logarithm Problem structure, the authors show that classical AI fails because it cannot access the latent feature required to determine repair feasibility. Quantum algorithms are necessary here not as a general speedup, but as a specific tool to unlock a hidden piece of information needed for correct decision-making.
main_problem: The paper addresses the rigidity of hard-constraint decision systems that issue false vetoes for candidates that could be made feasible through an affordable repair.
main_result: A quantum-enhanced framework (Q-RACL) successfully implements a 'repair-before-veto' semantics, demonstrating that quantum access to latent features is necessary to keep the false-veto rate below 1.1%.
method: The authors construct a decision problem hidden within a Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) structure and compare classical policies against a quantum policy utilizing Fourier/Shor-like feature access.
model_or_system: The core model is a Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning (RACL) framework implemented via a finite-horizon repair Markov Decision Process ($M_{rep}$). The difficulty is encoded by making the necessary 'repair class' dependent on a latent exponent $a$ in a DLP, which is only accessible quantumly.
key_observables: False-Veto Rate (FVR) and Conditional Quantum Necessity Index ($	ext{QNIcond}$), both used to quantify the success of avoiding incorrect rejections.
important_parameters: The performance is tested across six primes ($p$) and ten seeds, demonstrating robustness in the DLP setting.
main_assumptions: The results rely on standard hardness assumptions regarding the difficulty of solving discrete logarithms classically versus quantumly.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate that bounded raw-input classical policies and incorrect Fourier encodings yield high FVRs, whereas the Q-DLP policy maintains a low FVR (< 1.1%), proving feature access is key.
paper_structure: The paper first defines the RACL semantics to overcome veto rigidity. It then maps this problem onto a DLP-hidden structure. The core contribution involves comparing classical policies against the quantum Q-DLP policy, showing that only the latter can correctly infer the necessary repair link.
why_interesting: While not directly about AMO physics, the methodology of encoding complex physical constraints (like feasibility checks in quantum systems) into computationally hard mathematical structures (DLP) and demonstrating a quantum advantage for feature extraction is highly relevant to developing quantum machine learning tools for condensed matter simulations or quantum control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Q-RACL as one framework. RACL supplies the repair-before-veto semantics: repair operators, budgets, preference gates, credits, and plans. The DLP-hidden subfamily makes the repair-feasibility inference link depend on a latent discrete-log coordinate. Quantum feature access is localized to this link.

Figure 2: Main empirical pattern. Bounded raw-input classical policies and the wrong raw-Fourier encoding remain high-FVR, while the Q-DLP policy stays below 1.1% FVR. Exact six-prime values and QNIcond scores are reported in Table 2.
